question: How does the flexural strength reported for the continuous carbon fiber-reinforced ZrB2-based composite fabricated by Liu et al. (2021) via electrophoretic deposition plus hot pressing compare to the oxidation resistance limit reported by Didenko and Astapov (2025) for Cf/ZrB2-SiC composites processed by slurry infiltration and polymer infiltration/pyrolysis?
reference_answer: Liu et al. (2021) measured a flexural strength of 199 ± 26 MPa for their EPD plus hot-pressed continuous Cf/ZrB2-based composite, while Didenko and Astapov (2025) report that Cf/ZrB2-SiC composites made by slurry infiltration and PIP retain high oxidation and ablation resistance up to about 2000 degrees C before degrading further at 2000-2800 degrees C. The two papers address different performance metrics (mechanical strength versus thermal oxidation limit) for related ZrB2-based fiber-reinforced ceramics.
evidence_any: The Cf/ZrB2-based composite in the current work exhibited a flexural strength of 199
Cf/ZrB2-SiC has high oxidation and ablation resistance up to 2000 C
excerpts: A process combining electrophoretic deposition (EPD) with hot pressing (HP) was developed to fabricate continuous carbon fiber-reinforced ZrB2-based composites (Cf/ZrB2-based composites). ZrB2-based ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC) particles were uniformly pre-coated on continuous carbon fibers via EPD. Then, the UHTC-coated carbon fibers were stacked and hot pressed to prepare the Cf/ZrB2-based composites. Microstructure observations revealed that almost no micro-pores were found in the inter-bundle and intra-bundle regions of fibers after HP. The flexural strength, fracture toughness and the work of fracture of the Cf/ZrB2-based composite were measured as 199 ± 26 MPa, 6.71 ± 1.29 MPa m1/2, and 754 ± 58 J/m2, respectively. Based on the observations of non-brittle fracture behavior, fractured morphology and crack propagation, the enhanced fracture properties were mainly attributed to the multiple toughening mechanisms, such as fiber pull-out, fiber bridging, crack deflection and branching along the interfaces. The Cf/ZrB2-based composite in the current work exhibited a flexural strength of 199 ± 26 MPa. Similar strength values were obtained for ZrB2-based composites reinforced by continuous C and SiC fibers, and from other carbon fiber-reinforced UHTC-based composites.

The introduction of ZrB2 into Cf/SiC-ZrB2 composites significantly enhanced their ablation performance compared to Cf/SiC. This is because the heterophase matrix is able to withstand higher temperatures, due to the refractoriness of ZrB2. Additionally, the heterophase matrix exhibits a reduced tendency to high temperature decomposition and has superior anti-oxidation properties. Wang et al. concluded that Cf/ZrB2-SiC has high oxidation and ablation resistance up to 2000 C, and a relatively low ablation rate in the temperature range of 2000-2800 C in an oxyacetylene flame for 20 s. And Mungiguerra et al. demonstrated that UHTCMC with a ZrB2-based matrix exhibited promising behavior under harsh conditions typical of hybrid rocket operation. The composites demonstrated superior erosion resistance compared to benchmark state-of-art materials. To evaluate the long-term ablation behavior, Zhang et al. exposed 3D Cf/ZrB2-SiC composites to an oxyacetylene flame for varying periods of time. After ablation at 2465 C for 100 s, the composites were coated with three-layer oxide films resulting from coarsening of the ZrO2 phase and transformation of the glassy SiO2 phase into dense and porous layers, with the outermost porous ZrO2 layer resulting from the active oxidation of SiC.

question: What processing route did Liu et al. (2021) use to fabricate their continuous carbon fiber-reinforced ZrB2-based composite, and how does it differ from the chemical vapor infiltration, polymer infiltration and pyrolysis, reactive melt infiltration, and slurry infiltration routes that Didenko and Astapov (2025) identify as the most commonly used technologies for producing UHTC matrix composites?
reference_answer: Liu et al. (2021) combined electrophoretic deposition (EPD) with hot pressing to coat continuous carbon fibers with ZrB2-based UHTC particles before densification, whereas Didenko and Astapov (2025) identify chemical vapor infiltration/deposition, polymer infiltration and pyrolysis, reactive melt infiltration, and slurry infiltration as the most commonly used technologies for producing UHTC ceramic matrices. EPD combined with hot pressing is therefore a distinct route from the four dominant matrix-forming technologies surveyed in the review.
evidence_any: combining electrophoretic deposition (EPD) with hot pressing (HP) was developed to fabricate continuous carbon fiber-reinforced ZrB2-based composites
chemical vapor infiltration/deposition (CVI/CVD), polymer infiltration and pyrolysis (PIP), reactive melt infiltration (RMI), and slurry infiltration (SI)
excerpts: A process combining electrophoretic deposition (EPD) with hot pressing (HP) was developed to fabricate continuous carbon fiber-reinforced ZrB2-based composites (Cf/ZrB2-based composites). ZrB2-based ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC) particles were uniformly pre-coated on continuous carbon fibers via EPD. Then, the UHTC-coated carbon fibers were stacked and hot pressed to prepare the Cf/ZrB2-based composites. Microstructure observations revealed that almost no micro-pores were found in the inter-bundle and intra-bundle regions of fibers after HP. The flexural strength, fracture toughness and the work of fracture of the Cf/ZrB2-based composite were measured as 199 ± 26 MPa, 6.71 ± 1.29 MPa m1/2, and 754 ± 58 J/m2, respectively. Based on the observations of non-brittle fracture behavior, fractured morphology and crack propagation, the enhanced fracture properties were mainly attributed to the multiple toughening mechanisms, such as fiber pull-out, fiber bridging, crack deflection and branching along the interfaces. The Cf/ZrB2-based composite in the current work exhibited a flexural strength of 199 ± 26 MPa. Similar strength values were obtained for ZrB2-based composites reinforced by continuous C and SiC fibers, and from other carbon fiber-reinforced UHTC-based composites.

The design and structuring of composite reinforcements, such as the introduction of nanowires or rods, or giving them a special structure, also have a significant influence on the structure and phase composition of the final product and, as a consequence, on its behavior at high temperatures. In recent years, significant progress has been made in the development of UHTCMCs manufacturing technologies. A critical analysis of these achievements is presented in detail in the reviews, so there is no point in repeating them in this article. The most used technologies for producing ceramic matrices are chemical vapor infiltration/deposition (CVI/CVD), polymer infiltration and pyrolysis (PIP), reactive melt infiltration (RMI), and slurry infiltration (SI). A significant part of the research is devoted to a review of less common methods for obtaining UHTCMCs, such as hot pressing (HP), spark plasma sintering (SPS), in situ reaction, sol-gel infiltration, powder pre-infiltration (PPI), as well as the use of combined technologies.

question: What toughening or crack-resistance mechanisms are emphasized by Liu et al. (2021) for their continuous Cf/ZrB2-based composite compared to the densification-related porosity problems that Didenko and Astapov (2025) describe for Cf/CeZrC composites prepared by controlled liquid vapor deposition (CLVD)?
reference_answer: Liu et al. (2021) attribute the enhanced fracture properties of their Cf/ZrB2-based composite to multiple toughening mechanisms including fiber pull-out and fiber bridging, achieved through a nearly pore-free microstructure from the EPD plus hot-pressing route. Didenko and Astapov (2025) instead describe how poor densification behavior in the CLVD process for Cf/CeZrC composites leads to high porosity and low ceramic content, which degrades ablation resistance rather than improving toughness.
evidence_any: The enhanced fracture properties could be associated with the multiple toughening mechanisms, including fiber pull-out, fiber bridging
the poor densification behavior produced by the high temperature in CLVD process, which resulted in a low density
excerpts: Continuous Cf/ZrB2-based composites were fabricated by combined electrophoretic deposition and sintering processes, where the electrophoretic deposition (EPD) was developed to form the green bodies and the hot pressing (HP) was employed to densify the composites. The EPD process can deposit UHTC particles on continuous carbon fibers and control the thickness of UHTC coating by changing the process time. Thanks to the uniform UHTC coatings on the carbon fibers, after hot pressing, no pores on a micro-scale level were observed in the continuous Cf/ZrB2-based composite. The good mechanical properties could be attributed to the intact fibers and the suitable fiber-matrix bonding interface. The enhanced fracture properties could be associated with the multiple toughening mechanisms, including fiber pull-out, fiber bridging, crack deflection and branching along the interfaces. The X-ray diffraction patterns for the UHTC-coated carbon fibers are presented in Fig. 2. For the carbon fibers pre-treated with PDA, a broaden peak associated with the (002) diffraction plane appeared at about 25.5 degrees. Continuous carbon fibers (T700, supplied by Toray Industries, Japan) were used as the reinforcing materials for the fabrication of composites. ZrB2 and ZrSi2 were used as the raw UHTC powders (75 vol.% ZrB2 + 25 vol.% ZrSi2).

The results showed that the mass gain, densification rate, and density of the samples increased when the deposition temperature was elevated from 800 to 900 C and then decreased with further temperature increase. Furthermore, the homogenous distribution of ZrC obtained by the CLVD at 900 C provided the formation of a compact ZrO2 layer, which inhibited the mass transfer of oxidizing agents and enhanced the resistance to mechanical denudation. The linear and mass ablation rates of the composite obtained at 900 C were found to be 62.52 and 70.77 percent lower, respectively, than those of the composite obtained at 1100 C. These phenomena are primarily attributed to the poor densification behavior produced by the high temperature in CLVD process, which resulted in a low density, poor ZrC ceramic content and its uneven distribution. Li et al. demonstrated that the ablation properties of Cf/CeSiCeZrC composites were influenced by the RMI process temperature, which significantly impacted their microstructure. The linear and mass ablation rates of the composites decreased with an increase in the RMI temperature from 2000 to 2300 C.

question: How does the near-pore-free microstructure achieved by Liu et al. (2021) in continuous Cf/ZrB2-based composites via EPD contrast with the porosity increase Didenko and Astapov (2025) describe for PIP-prepared Cf/ZrC composites synthesized at higher temperatures?
reference_answer: Liu et al. (2021) found that combining electrophoretic deposition with hot pressing produced almost no micro-pores in the inter-bundle and intra-bundle fiber regions of their continuous Cf/ZrB2-based composite. In contrast, Didenko and Astapov (2025) note that as the PIP preparation temperature for Cf/ZrC composites increased, the open porosity of the composites increased, allowing greater oxidant transport and weakening ablation resistance.
evidence_any: almost no micro-pores were found in the inter-bundle and intra-bundle regions of fibers after HP
As the preparation temperature increased, the open porosity of the composites increased, allowing greater oxidant
excerpts: Microstructure and mechanical properties of continuous carbon fiber-reinforced ZrB2-based composites via combined electrophoretic deposition and sintering. A process combining electrophoretic deposition (EPD) with hot pressing (HP) was developed to fabricate continuous carbon fiber-reinforced ZrB2-based composites (Cf/ZrB2-based composites). ZrB2-based ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC) particles were uniformly pre-coated on continuous carbon fibers via EPD. Then, the UHTC-coated carbon fibers were stacked and hot pressed to prepare the Cf/ZrB2-based composites. Microstructure observations revealed that almost no micro-pores were found in the inter-bundle and intra-bundle regions of fibers after HP. The flexural strength, fracture toughness and the work of fracture of the Cf/ZrB2-based composite were measured as 199 ± 26 MPa, 6.71 ± 1.29 MPa m1/2, and 754 ± 58 J/m2, respectively. Based on the observations of non-brittle fracture behavior, fractured morphology and crack propagation, the enhanced fracture properties were mainly attributed to the multiple toughening mechanisms, such as fiber pull-out, fiber bridging, crack deflection and branching along the interfaces.

During the oxidation process in an oxyacetylene flame, a gas-tight oxide layer consisting of SiO2 and ZrO2 was formed on the surface. Li et al. evaluated ablation properties of PIP-prepared Cf/ZrC composites under oxyacetylene flame with a heat flux of 4.187 MW/m2 for 60 s depending on the synthesis temperature (1500, 1550, or 1600 C). As the preparation temperature increased, the open porosity of the composites increased, allowing greater oxidant transport into the composite volume, which in turn weakened the ablation resistance. So, the authors concluded that the optimal preparation temperature is 1500 C. Li et al. also investigated the dependence of Cf/CeZrC ablation properties on different values of solution pH during microwave hydrothermal treatment, which was part of the composite preparation process. The linear ablation rate of Cf/CeZrC prepared at pH 13 exhibited a marked reduction compared to composites prepared at pH 4 and pH 7. Li et al. investigated the dependence of Cf/CeZrC ablation properties on different values of solution pH during microwave hydrothermal treatment, which was part of the composite preparation process involving carbothermal reduction of hydrothermal ZrO2, finding that a higher pH improved the uniform dispersion of the ZrC particles and reduced the ablation rate.

question: What surface temperatures were achieved on ZrB2-WC-SiC ceramic buttons in the supersonic arc-jet tests of Silvestroni et al. (2019), and how do these compare with the temperature range over which Didenko and Astapov (2025) say the glassy SiO2 protective layer on ZrB2-SiC composites remains stable before volatilizing or peeling off?
reference_answer: Silvestroni et al. (2019) tested ZrB2-WC-SiC buttons in a supersonic arc-jet wind tunnel and achieved surface temperatures up to 2650 K in simulated air and 2800 K in pure nitrogen. Didenko and Astapov (2025) report that the glassy SiO2 layer protecting Cf/ZrB2-SiC composites is stable up to about 1850 degrees C, after which it volatilizes or peels off, a lower threshold than the peak temperatures reached in the Silvestroni arc-jet tests.
evidence_any: achieving temperatures up to 2650 and 2800 K
This glassy SiO2 gas-tight oxide layer protected the composites from ablation up to 1850 C
excerpts: The objective of this study is thus to investigate the oxidation behavior of a very high strength ZrB2-based ceramic under high-enthalpy supersonic flow with two different chemical compositions. Therefore, two ZrB2-SiC-WC coupons were exposed to an arc-jet flow composed of simulated air, with oxygen partial pressure in the order of thousands of Pa, or pure nitrogen, with oxygen residual partial pressure two orders of magnitude lower, achieving temperatures up to 2650 and 2800 K, respectively. Microstructural analysis enabled to propose the mechanism underpinning the oxidation behavior of this system in extreme environment containing diverse fractions of oxygen. A ZrB2 - 5 vol% SiC - 5 vol% WC ceramic pellet was sintered to full density according to the methodology described in a previous work. From the sintered disc, two flat button-shaped coupons were machined with a 5 mm-thick head, 17 mm diameter and an overall length of 10 mm including the cylindrical rear pin.

After ablation at 2465 C for 100 s, the composites were coated with three-layer oxide films: a dense ZrO2 + SiO2 layer resulted from the coarsening of the ZrO2 phase and transformation of the glassy SiO2 phase, a porous ZrO2 + SiO2 layer owing to the limited molten SiO2 phases, and a porous ZrO2 layer resulted from the active oxidation of SiC. The formation of these materials is described as follows. As the operating temperature approached 1300 C or above, the glassy SiO2 phase formed by passive oxidation of SiC particles accumulated under capillary forces. This glassy SiO2 gas-tight oxide layer protected the composites from ablation up to 1850 C. With a further increase in temperature, the glassy SiO2 layer was either volatilized or peeled off. The losses were compensated by the upwelling of the SiO2 internal phase under the action of capillary forces and high SiO vapor pressure, forming a dense layer of ZrO2 + SiO2 on the surface and a porous layer under it. Thereafter, SiC was subjected to active oxidation, resulting in the formation of a SiC-depleted layer.

question: How does the temperature jump phenomenon observed by Silvestroni et al. (2019) during arc-jet testing of ZrB2-WC-SiC ceramics relate to the beneficial role of oxygen partial pressure that Didenko and Astapov (2025) attribute to protective oxide film formation on carbon-ceramic composites in general?
reference_answer: Silvestroni et al. (2019) observed temperature jumps of 500-600 K in both simulated air and nitrogen flows during arc-jet testing of ZrB2-WC-SiC ceramics, and found that the presence of oxygen in the incoming flow was beneficial for retarding material consumption by enabling formation of a protective silica glass layer. Didenko and Astapov (2025) similarly emphasize that formation of protective, passivating oxide films on carbon-ceramic UHTC composites slows mass transfer of reagents and improves resistance to erosion and denudation.
evidence_any: Temperature jumps of 500-600 K were observed in both environments at constant flow conditions
formation and evolution of passivating heterogeneous oxide films, which are represented mainly by the refractory MeO2 phase
excerpts: A super-strong ZrB2 ceramic containing WC and SiC was tested in a supersonic arc-jet wind tunnel by exposure to flows with two chemical compositions, simulated air or pure nitrogen, at temperatures of 2650 and 2800 K. Temperature jumps of 500-600 K were observed in both environments at constant flow conditions. SEM analyses revealed that oxygen in the high-enthalpy flow retards the material consumption owing to the formation of partially protective glass. Then, it appears that even 5 vol% of W-compounds is sufficient to modify the subscale oxide configuration and form a cell-like multi-layered architecture typical of tungsten, rather than that typical of ZrB2. Substantial advancements in hypersonic flight technology and development of new low-cost space launch systems are possible only if new materials able to meet the requirements of such demanding thermal loadings are developed. Hypersonic flight vehicles with velocities above Mach 5 experience extremely high aerodynamic and thermo-chemical loads that can seriously impact vehicle structural integrity.

A review and critical analysis of recent advances in the field of oxidation and ablation resistance of carbon-ceramic composite materials, which are the most promising for high temperature applications in load-bearing structures and heat-protective systems of rocket and aerospace engineering, is carried out. The focus of this study is on the behavior of Cf/UHTC, Cf/CeUHTC, Cf/SiCeUHTC, and Cf/CeSiCeUHTC composites under thermochemical interaction with oxidizing gas flows. The workability of the composites is provided by the formation and evolution of passivating heterogeneous oxide films, which are represented mainly by the refractory MeO2 phase and the glass phase modified by Me4+ cations (Me being Zr and/or Hf). The protective oxide layers slow down the mass transfer of reagents, due to the high gas density caused by the presence of phases in a viscous-fluid state, and resist mechanical erosion and denudation, due to the framework structure provided by the partial sintering of refractory phase grains.

question: How does the effect of a small volume fraction of W-compounds on the sub-surface oxide architecture of ZrB2-based ceramics, as reported by Silvestroni et al. (2019), compare to the effect of ZrB2 particle addition on the ablation resistance of Cf/C composites discussed by Didenko and Astapov (2025)?
reference_answer: Silvestroni et al. (2019) found that even 5 vol% of W-compounds in a ZrB2-SiC-WC ceramic is sufficient to alter the sub-scale oxide configuration into a multi-layered cell-like architecture typical of tungsten oxidation. Didenko and Astapov (2025) report a different, beneficial additive effect: ZrB2 particle addition to Cf/C composites significantly improved ablation resistance because the molten ZrO2 formed hinders oxygen diffusion.
evidence_any: even 5 vol% of W-compounds is sufficient to modify the subscale oxide configuration
ZrB2 addition to the Cf/C composites significantly improved the ablation resistance of the materials
excerpts: A super-strong ZrB2 ceramic containing WC and SiC was tested in a supersonic arc-jet wind tunnel by exposure to flows with two chemical compositions, simulated air or pure nitrogen, at temperatures of 2650 and 2800 K. Temperature jumps of 500-600 K were observed in both environments at constant flow conditions. SEM analyses revealed that oxygen in the high-enthalpy flow retards the material consumption owing to the formation of partially protective glass. Then, it appears that even 5 vol% of W-compounds is sufficient to modify the subscale oxide configuration and form a cell-like multi-layered architecture typical of tungsten, rather than that typical of ZrB2. Substantial advancements in hypersonic flight technology and development of new low-cost space launch systems are possible only if new materials able to meet the requirements of such demanding thermal loadings are developed. Hypersonic flight vehicles with velocities above Mach 5 experience extremely high aerodynamic and thermo-chemical loads that can seriously impact vehicle structural integrity.

One potential method to enhance the oxidation resistance and thermal shock resistance of UHTCs is to introduce large quantities of ZrB2- or HfB2-based ceramic particles into Cf/C composites. This approach is proposed due to the high melting points of ZrB2 and HfB2 coupled with the ability to form refractory oxide scales capable to withstand temperatures up to 2500 C. Tang et al. determined that the ZrB2 addition to the Cf/C composites significantly improved the ablation resistance of the materials under a 3.92 MW/m2 heat flux. The improved behavior was mainly attributed to the molten ZrO2 hindering the oxygen diffusion. The combination of low density, good mechanical properties, thermal shock and high temperature oxidation resistance demonstrated by Cf/C composites with HfB2 particles clearly highlighted their potential for hypersonic applications. The formation of a protective layer during arc-jet test favored the performance of the composite.

question: How does the core-shell (Zr,W)B2 microstructure of the ZrB2-WC-SiC ceramic studied by Silvestroni et al. (2019) relate to the flexural strength values Liu et al. (2021) report for continuous carbon fiber-reinforced ZrB2-based composites made without WC additions?
reference_answer: Silvestroni et al. (2019) studied a ZrB2-WC-SiC ceramic whose boride matrix displayed a core-shell substructure with ZrB2 as the core and a (Zr,W)B2 solid solution as the shell, giving it very high strength retention at elevated temperature. Liu et al. (2021) instead studied a continuous carbon fiber-reinforced ZrB2-ZrSi2 composite without WC, measuring a lower flexural strength of 199 ± 26 MPa, reflecting the different reinforcement strategy (fiber toughening versus solid-solution core-shell strengthening).
evidence_any: displaying the typical core-shell substructure, with ZrB2 being the core and a (Zr,W)B2 solid solution the shell
The Cf/ZrB2-based composite in the current work exhibited a flexural strength of 199
excerpts: The microstructure of this ceramic has been extensively studied and described in previous papers. To briefly summarize the main aspects and to understand the microstructure variation upon the arc-jet flow tests, the boride matrix had mean grain size around 1.8 micrometers displaying the typical core-shell substructure, with ZrB2 being the core and a (Zr,W)B2 solid solution the shell. The secondary phases were homogeneously distributed: SiC is recognizable with dark contrast, WB has white aspect, whilst residual WC is light grey. The formation of the WB phase is due to the chemical reaction between the initial WC and B2O3 covering the diboride powder particles. The amount of these secondary phases was estimated by image analysis to be about 5 vol% SiC, about 4.9 vol% WB and less than 1 vol% of WC. In light of these experiments simulating the hypersonic environment, it seems that the microstructure evolution under air or nitrogen flow is alike, but a larger amount of oxygen in the case of air flow is actually beneficial to boost a healing mechanism by formation of a glassy outermost phase which partially mitigates or retards the material degradation.

A process combining electrophoretic deposition (EPD) with hot pressing (HP) was developed to fabricate continuous carbon fiber-reinforced ZrB2-based composites (Cf/ZrB2-based composites). ZrB2-based ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC) particles were uniformly pre-coated on continuous carbon fibers via EPD. Then, the UHTC-coated carbon fibers were stacked and hot pressed to prepare the Cf/ZrB2-based composites. Based on the observations of non-brittle fracture behavior, fractured morphology and crack propagation, the enhanced fracture properties were mainly attributed to the multiple toughening mechanisms, such as fiber pull-out, fiber bridging, crack deflection and branching along the interfaces. The Cf/ZrB2-based composite in the current work exhibited a flexural strength of 199 ± 26 MPa. Similar strength values were obtained for ZrB2-based composites reinforced by continuous C and SiC fibers, and from other carbon fiber-reinforced UHTC-based composites.

question: What role does silicon carbide play in the oxidation resistance of the ZrB2-WC-SiC ceramic tested by Silvestroni et al. (2019) in an arc-jet wind tunnel, compared to the role of SiC discussed by Didenko and Astapov (2025) in the general oxidation mechanism of Cf/ZrB2-SiC composites?
reference_answer: Silvestroni et al. (2019) found that in their ZrB2-WC-SiC ceramic, silicon carbide forms an outermost SiO2 glassy coating that mitigates bursting phenomena during arc-jet exposure to air, providing a partially healing oxidation mechanism. Didenko and Astapov (2025) describe an analogous role for SiC in Cf/ZrB2-SiC composites, where passive oxidation of SiC forms a glassy SiO2 gas-tight oxide layer that protects the composite from ablation up to about 1850 degrees C before it eventually volatilizes.
evidence_any: formation of surface silica-based glass mitigated bursting phenomena
This glassy SiO2 gas-tight oxide layer protected the composites from ablation up to 1850 C
excerpts: The comparison of the two test specimens revealed analogous oxide architectures, generally characterized by WOx-based vapors in the sub-scales, with particular effervescence in the case of the sample exposed to nitrogen flow. In the sample exposed to air, formation of surface silica-based glass mitigated bursting phenomena. The solid crystalline compounds, ZrO2, W and WO3 were present in a wide spectrum of morphologies, testifying large partial pressure variation from point-to-point. A volcano-model was proposed to explain the oxidation mechanism of this system in the absence of a protecting glassy silica layer, involving the cyclic accumulation of gases at high pressure in the sub-scales and eruption of fluids. Two major conclusions were drawn. First, it seems that presence of oxygen in the incoming flow is beneficial for retarding the material consumption, thanks to the activation of partially healing mechanism by SiC oxidation and B2O3 retention into a protective silica glass. Second, it seems that introduction of W-compounds in the present concentration, 5 vol%, although fundamental for ZrB2 strength retention above 2000 C, dictates the formation of the sub-surface architecture making it resemble that of pure W.

After ablation at 2465 C for 100 s, the composites were coated with three-layer oxide films: a dense ZrO2 + SiO2 layer resulted from the coarsening of the ZrO2 phase and transformation of the glassy SiO2 phase, a porous ZrO2 + SiO2 layer owing to the limited molten SiO2 phases, and a porous ZrO2 layer resulted from the active oxidation of SiC. The formation of these materials is described as follows. As the operating temperature approached 1300 C or above, the glassy SiO2 phase formed by passive oxidation of SiC particles accumulated under capillary forces. This glassy SiO2 gas-tight oxide layer protected the composites from ablation up to 1850 C. With a further increase in temperature, the glassy SiO2 layer was either volatilized or peeled off. The losses were compensated by the upwelling of the SiO2 internal phase under the action of capillary forces and high SiO vapor pressure, forming a dense layer of ZrO2 + SiO2 on the surface and a porous layer under it. Thereafter, SiC was subjected to active oxidation, resulting in the formation of a SiC-depleted layer.

question: How do the crack-tolerance and toughening mechanisms reported by Liu et al. (2021) for continuous Cf/ZrB2-based composites compare to the ablation performance data Didenko and Astapov (2025) report for 3D Cf/ZrB2-SiC composites exposed to oxyacetylene torch flames with different heat fluxes?
reference_answer: Liu et al. (2021) attribute the enhanced mechanical toughness of their continuous Cf/ZrB2-based composite to fiber pull-out, fiber bridging, crack deflection and branching enabled by a clean fiber-matrix interface. Didenko and Astapov (2025) report that Cf/ZrB2-SiC composites were separately evaluated for ablation properties under oxyacetylene torch flames with different heat fluxes, showing that ablation performance and mechanical toughness are studied as distinct characteristics of these ZrB2-based composite systems.
evidence_any: The enhanced fracture properties could be associated with the multiple toughening mechanisms, including fiber pull-out, fiber bridging
The ablation properties of Cf/CeZrB2eSiC composite were studied under an oxyacetylene torch flame with different heat fluxes
excerpts: Continuous Cf/ZrB2-based composites were fabricated by combined electrophoretic deposition and sintering processes, where the electrophoretic deposition (EPD) was developed to form the green bodies and the hot pressing (HP) was employed to densify the composites. The EPD process can deposit UHTC particles on continuous carbon fibers and control the thickness of UHTC coating by changing the process time. Thanks to the uniform UHTC coatings on the carbon fibers, after hot pressing, no pores on a micro-scale level were observed in the continuous Cf/ZrB2-based composite. The good mechanical properties could be attributed to the intact fibers and the suitable fiber-matrix bonding interface. The enhanced fracture properties could be associated with the multiple toughening mechanisms, including fiber pull-out, fiber bridging, crack deflection and branching along the interfaces. The X-ray diffraction patterns for the UHTC-coated carbon fibers are presented in Fig. 2. For the carbon fibers pre-treated with PDA, a broaden peak associated with the (002) diffraction plane appeared at about 25.5 degrees. Continuous carbon fibers (T700, supplied by Toray Industries, Japan) were used as the reinforcing materials for the fabrication of composites. ZrB2 and ZrSi2 were used as the raw UHTC powders (75 vol.% ZrB2 + 25 vol.% ZrSi2).

Reactions are expected to occur in the ablation process: ZrB2(s) plus 2.5O2(g) forms ZrO2(s) plus B2O3(l); B2O3(l) forms B2O3(g). The ablation properties of Cf/CeZrB2eSiC composite were studied under an oxyacetylene torch flame with different heat fluxes. The ablation resistance decreased with increasing heat flux from 2.4 to 4.2 MW/m2, and the ablation mechanism changed from chemical erosion to mechanical denudation. The high ablation resistance at 2.4 MW/m2 was attributed to the formation of the SiO2-ZrO2 mixture. At a heat flux of 3.2 MW/m2, corresponding to a temperature above 2300 C, the formation of a viscous-fluid ZrO2 partially prevented oxygen diffusion and provided protection against ablation. But as the heat flux increased further, the concentration of thermal energy intensified denudation, leading to cracking and spalling of the protective oxide scale and an increase in the ablation rate of the Cf/CeZrB2eSiC composite, showing that ablation resistance depends strongly on the applied heat flux.

question: How does the tungsten-rich sub-surface architecture observed by Silvestroni et al. (2019) after arc-jet exposure of ZrB2-WC-SiC ceramics compare to the densification-porosity relationship Didenko and Astapov (2025) describe for Cf/CeZrC composites made by CLVD at different deposition temperatures?
reference_answer: Silvestroni et al. (2019) found that even a modest 5 vol% of W-compounds in a ZrB2-WC-SiC ceramic causes the sub-scale oxide to develop a multi-layered, cell-like architecture typical of tungsten oxidation rather than plain ZrB2. excerpts: A super-strong ZrB2 ceramic containing WC and SiC was tested in a supersonic arc-jet wind tunnel by exposure to flows with two chemical compositions, simulated air or pure nitrogen, at temperatures of 2650 and 2800 K. Temperature jumps of 500-600 K were observed in both environments at constant flow conditions. SEM analyses revealed that oxygen in the high-enthalpy flow retards the material consumption owing to the formation of partially protective glass. Then, it appears that even 5 vol% of W-compounds is sufficient to modify the subscale oxide configuration and form a cell-like multi-layered architecture typical of tungsten, rather than that typical of ZrB2. Substantial advancements in hypersonic flight technology and development of new low-cost space launch systems are possible only if new materials able to meet the requirements of such demanding thermal loadings are developed. Hypersonic flight vehicles with velocities above Mach 5 experience extremely high aerodynamic and thermo-chemical loads that can seriously impact vehicle structural integrity.

The results showed that the mass gain, densification rate, and density of the samples increased when the deposition temperature was elevated from 800 to 900 C and then decreased with further temperature increase. Furthermore, the homogenous distribution of ZrC obtained by the CLVD at 900 C provided the formation of a compact ZrO2 layer, which inhibited the mass transfer of oxidizing agents and enhanced the resistance to mechanical denudation. The linear and mass ablation rates of the composite obtained at 900 C were found to be 62.52 and 70.77 percent lower, respectively, than those of the composite obtained at 1100 C. These phenomena are primarily attributed to the poor densification behavior produced by the high temperature in CLVD process, which resulted in a low density, poor ZrC ceramic content and its uneven distribution. Li et al. demonstrated that the ablation properties of Cf/CeSiCeZrC composites were influenced by the RMI process temperature, which significantly impacted their microstructure. The linear and mass ablation rates of the composites decreased with an increase in the RMI temperature from 2000 to 2300 C.
